title: Exploration vs Exploitation
concept: An agent constantly faces a trade-off: exploit the action it already knows works well, or explore a new action that might work even better.

exercises.prompt: Recap: How does an agent's behavior usually change after many trials of rewards and penalties?

options.text: Good actions happen more often, bad ones fade out
Nothing changes no matter how many trials happen
The agent stops taking any actions

exercises.explain: Right! Repeated reward signals gradually shift the agent toward better actions.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A single reward is usually enough to fully teach an agent the best behavior.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! It takes many trials of rewards and penalties for behavior to reliably shift.

cards.title: The Same Noodle Shop, Every Friday
cards.body: You know a noodle shop that's always good — you order the same dish every Friday and you're always happy. Two blocks away, a new ramen place just opened. It might be even better... or it might be a letdown. Do you play it safe, or take the risk?

cards.title: Two competing strategies
cards.body: Exploitation means picking the action already known to give a good reward. Exploration means trying a new, untested action to see if it might pay off even better. An agent needs both — too much of either one holds it back.

cards.title: Favorite restaurant vs. trying somewhere new
cards.body: Always eating at your favorite restaurant (exploiting) guarantees a meal you know you'll enjoy — but you'll never discover if a new spot is even better. Always trying somewhere new (exploring) means you might find a hidden gem, or you might end up with several disappointing meals along the way.

cards.title: Two vending machines, one better than the other
cards.body: Imagine two snack machines. One has given you a good snack every time you've tried it. The other you've never used — it could be amazing or terrible. Sticking only with the known machine is exploitation. Testing the mystery machine, even though the first one already works, is exploration.

cards.title: Exploring isn't wasted effort
cards.body: It's tempting to think exploring is just a mistake or a distraction from "the real goal." It's not — without ever exploring, an agent can get stuck repeating a decent-but-not-great action forever, never discovering something better nearby.

left.text:
Reliable reward
from what's known to work

right.text:
Uncertain outcome,
chance of a bigger reward

exercises.prompt: Choosing your usual go-to lunch spot because you know you'll enjoy it is an example of:

options.text: Exploitation
Exploration
A penalty

exercises.explain: Right! Sticking with what's already known to be good is exploitation.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Exploration means trying an action the agent hasn't tested much, even though the outcome is uncertain.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! Exploration is about testing the unknown to see if it pays off.

exercises.sentence: Sticking with the action already known to work well is called ___, while trying something new is called exploration.
exercises.explain: Exploitation means leaning on the action you already know gives a good reward.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best describes exploration rather than exploitation?

options.text: A music app occasionally recommends a totally new artist you've never listened to, instead of only your usual favorites
A music app only ever plays the exact songs you've already rated 5 stars
A calendar app reminds you of an appointment

exercises.explain: Right! Recommending something untested, even at the risk of a miss, is exploration.

exercises.prompt: A game-playing agent has found one strategy that reliably wins about 60% of matches. It has never tried a completely different strategy that could be better or worse. What's the risk of only exploiting the known strategy forever?

options.text: It may never discover a better strategy that was available all along
It will definitely start losing every match
There is no risk — exploiting is always the correct choice
The agent will stop receiving rewards altogether

exercises.explain: Exactly! Without any exploration, the agent can get stuck with a decent strategy while a better one goes undiscovered.

exercises.prompt: A food delivery app usually routes drivers along the same well-known road every time (fast and predictable), but occasionally sends a driver down a road it has less data on, which could be faster or could be slower. Why would the app intentionally do this sometimes?

options.text: To explore the less-known road so it might discover an even better route, instead of only exploiting the familiar one
Because the app is broken and picking roads at random
Because exploring guarantees a faster trip every single time
Because the well-known road is actually the environment, not an action

exercises.explain: Exactly! Occasionally exploring untested routes is how the app can discover something better than what it already exploits.

exercises.prompt: An agent playing a simple game has two buttons. Button A has been pressed 100 times and reliably gives a small reward. Button B has been pressed only twice, with mixed results. A purely exploit-only agent will press only Button A forever. What's the downside of that strategy?

options.text: The agent may never learn whether Button B could actually give a bigger reward, since it barely explored it
There is no downside — Button A is proven to work, so it's always the best long-term choice
Pressing Button A repeatedly counts as exploration
The agent would receive no reward at all from Button A

exercises.explain: Exactly! With only two tries, Button B is still mostly unknown — a purely exploit-only agent risks missing out on a better option it never gave a fair shot. Next: let's see reinforcement learning at work in games, robots, and the real world.
